Aldric had described speed. Marius had described patience. But Dorian had described something that undermined the very concept of free will, of cause and effect, of the logical structure of reality itself. Godric did not need to fight. He did not need to endure. He did not need to outrun. He simply existed, and existence arranged itself around his preferences like a servant smoothing the path before its master.

And Godric did not even notice. That was the throughline that Kaedan could not escape. Every wizard, across half a million years, had recorded the same terrible detail: Godric was genuinely unaware of the scale of his own power. He thought he was holding back. He thought he was being restrained. He thought his court wizards were overreacting.

"You have gone pale, my boy."

"Would you like some water?"

"I did not feel anything. Was it successful?"

"Things just seem to work out."

The warmth was real. The kindness was real. And the absolute, incomprehensible, reality-shattering power was also real. They were the same man. That was the horror.

Kaedan reached for the fifth journal. There was one more. He would read it. He had to read it. His father had told him to read them in order. There was a reason for the order.

---

**Journal of Valen — Year 789,000 — Senses**

My name is Valen. I am thirty-four years old. I have been Godric's Court Wizard for fourteen years. I am writing this journal entry with my eyes closed, because keeping them open reminds me of what I witnessed, and I need to separate myself from that memory if I am to set words to page at all.

The opposition did not have a name. I am not certain they understood the concept of names. They were entities—if one can call them entities—that existed in the absolute absence of perception. Not invisible. Not hidden. Unperceivable. They occupied the spaces between sensory input, the gaps between observation and reality, the silence between the notes of existence. They had never been detected by any being in the history of creation. They had never been seen, heard, felt, tasted, or smelled. They did not believe they could be perceived. They had built their entire existence on the absolute, unshakeable certainty that nothing in the omniverse possessed the sensory capacity to register their presence.

They attacked Godric from this state of assumed imperceptibility. Their attack was not physical, not temporal, not conceptual. It was a strike designed to sever the connection between Godric's consciousness and his physical form without him ever knowing the attack had occurred. They did not intend to fight him. They intended to assassinate him from a realm of existence that they believed was beyond his perception.

The arrogance of this assumption would be laughable if its consequences had not been so horrifying.

Godric possesses absolute omniscience. That is a fact that every citizen of Thronegate knows, but I am not certain that anyone truly understands what it means. It does not mean that Godric knows everything that exists. It means that Godric's sensory capacity—his ability to perceive, to detect, to register input—extends beyond existence itself. His senses stat, multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for nearly eight hundred thousand years, had reached a state where he could perceive things that did not exist, things that had never existed, things that existence itself had forgotten. The entities believed they were unperceivable. They were wrong. Godric perceived them more completely than they perceived themselves.

He did not look at them. He did not turn his head. He did not direct his attention toward them in any visible way. He simply noticed them, and his noticing was the most violent sensory act in the history of creation.

Godric's passive sensory perception, operating at effectively zero percent of its true capacity, registered the entities with such overwhelming completeness that the act of being perceived destroyed them. They had built their existence on not being perceived. Their entire being was predicated on the absence of observation. When Godric perceived them—when his infinite, omniscient senses registered their presence at a level of detail that exceeded their own self-awareness—their existence became a logical impossibility. They could not exist as unperceived entities while being perceived. The contradiction annihilated them.

But it was not a clean annihilation. Godric's senses did not merely register their presence; they registered their entire history, their entire nature, their every thought and impulse and weakness and fear. His perception flayed them open. It exposed every layer of their being to a scrutiny so complete, so absolute, so overwhelmingly intimate that their consciousness could not withstand the violation. They experienced being known—truly, completely, perfectly known—by a mind so vast that the act of knowing them was a form of conceptual violence. They were unmade not by force, but by the unbearable weight of being seen.

The omniverse did not escape this event unscathed. Godric's passive sensory field, briefly expressing itself against the entities, radiated outward and registered every single thing in existence with the same overwhelming completeness. Every atom. Every soul. Every thought. Every hidden secret and buried memory and forgotten dream across the entire omniverse was perceived, catalogued, known. For one infinitesimal instant, every being in creation experienced the sensation of being absolutely, perfectly observed by a consciousness that missed nothing, that forgot nothing, that registered everything. The collective psychic shock of that moment caused a wave of existential collapse across decillions of civilizations. Trillions of beings who harbored dark secrets, who had built their identities on carefully maintained deceptions, who had hidden their true natures from the world, were suddenly, violently exposed to the awareness that they had been seen. Completely. Irrevocably. Their masks were stripped away in an instant by the passive sensory overflow of a single god noticing a threat.

Godric restored the psychic damage in zero seconds, of course. He did not even seem aware that he had caused it. His senses returned to their normal, concealed state, and the omniverse resumed its existence as though nothing had happened. But the entities were not restored. They could not be restored. Their existence had been predicated on a condition—imperceptibility—that Godric's senses had permanently invalidated. They were erased from reality, their conceptual framework shattered by the simple fact that a being existed who could perceive them.

After it was over, Godric looked at me. His eyes were warm and kind, as they always are. "Valen," he said gently, "you are trembling. Is something the matter?"

I could not answer him. I could not form words. I had just been seen—truly, completely seen—by the same sensory field that had unmade the unperceivable entities. I had been known, for one eternal instant, in a way that no mortal being should ever be known. And the being who had known me was now asking if something was the matter, with genuine, innocent concern in his voice.

I am writing this so that whoever comes after me understands: Godric does not merely see you. He sees everything you are, everything you have been, everything you have hidden, everything you fear, everything you desire, and everything you have forgotten. He sees it all, constantly, passively, without effort. His omniscience is not a tool he uses. It is the air he breathes. The entities believed they were hidden. Nothing is hidden from him. Nothing has ever been hidden from him. Nothing will ever be hidden from him.

Yet he is kind. He is warm. He asks if you are well, and he means it. The King who perceives every flaw in your soul also genuinely cares about your comfort. This dissonance is the foundation of our service. We are known, completely known, by a being of infinite perception and infinite cruelty to his enemies and infinite kindness to his friends. We are his friends. We must never, ever become his enemies.

—Valen, Court Wizard of Thronegate, Year 789,000

---

Kaedan closed the fifth journal. His hands were shaking so violently that the leather cover rattled against the stone table. The metallic smell of ancestral dread was thick in the air, coating the back of his throat, suffocating him with its weight. Valen's journal was the worst of all of them. Valen had described what it felt like to be known, completely and perfectly known, by a consciousness that could perceive everything and forget nothing. Kaedan realized, with a sudden, sickening clarity, that Valen's description was not metaphor. It was literal. Godric had absolute omniscience. He knew everything about Kaedan. Every thought. Every fear. Every secret. And he was still kind. He was still warm. He still asked about his studies with genuine, avuncular affection.

That was the horror. That was the horror that ran through every journal, every entry, every trembling word written by his ancestors. Godric was not a tyrant who pretended to be kind. He was genuinely, authentically, completely kind—and he was also the being who had passively unmade the Adamant Colossus, retroactively erased the Chronovorous Swarm, outlasted the Eternal Famine without noticing, inverted the Probability Loom's calculations against them, and perceived the unperceivable entities into nonexistence. He was both. He was always both. The warmth and the annihilation were not contradictions. They were the same man, expressing different aspects of his infinite nature to different recipients.

The heavy oak door of the archive creaked open. Kaedan looked up, his vision blurry with unshed tears of existential dread, and saw his father standing in the doorway. Edrik's face was unreadable, but his eyes held something that Kaedan had never seen there before. It was understanding. It was compassion. It was the shared trauma of a bloodline that had served the apex predator of creation for a million years.

"You finished them," Edrik said. It was not a question.

"Yes," Kaedan whispered. His voice was hoarse, as though he had been screaming.

"Good." Edrik stepped into the archive and closed the door behind him. He walked to the table and sat down across from his son, his movements slow and deliberate, the movements of a man who had learned to move carefully in a world made of glass. "I know how you feel right now. I know the dread. I know the terror. I know the way your mind is trying to reconcile the Uncle Godric you have known your whole life with the being described in those journals. I know it feels impossible. I know it feels like your sanity is tearing apart at the seams."

"How do you bear it?" Kaedan asked. His voice cracked on the final word. "How do you serve him, knowing what he is?"

Edrik was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was very quiet. "Because he is both. He is the monster who unmakes civilizations with a passive glance, and he is the man who sat with me for three days after your mother died, not speaking, just being present, because he knew I needed silence and companionship more than words. He is the being whose luck stat can erase probability-manipulating civilizations from existence, and he is the king who remembers every birthday of every servant in the palace. He is the apex predator of creation, and he is my friend. Both things are true. Both things have always been true. The horror you feel right now is not a sign that your mind is breaking. It is the appropriate response to understanding the true nature of the being we serve."

Kaedan stared at the stack of journals on the table. Twenty-five thousand years of court wizards. Twenty-five thousand years of men who had witnessed the impossible and written it down so that others might be prepared. And they had all continued to serve. They had all continued to love.

"He does not understand," Kaedan said. "In every journal. He does not understand why they are afraid. He thinks they are overreacting."

Edrik nodded slowly. "Yes. That is perhaps the most terrifying thing of all. He genuinely believes he is being restrained. He genuinely believes the infinitesimal fractions of his power he allows us to witness are 'held back' and 'gentle.' He has told me, many times, that he finds our journals confusing. He says we are 'dramatic.' He says he always uses effectively zero percent of his power, and he does not understand why that upsets us."

Kaedan let out a sound that was somewhere between a laugh and a sob. "Zero percent. He uses zero percent of his power, with zero percent of his effort, and it annihilates the omniverse as collateral damage. And he thinks we are overreacting."

"Yes," Edrik said simply. "That is the nature of our service. We stand beside a being who could erase all of existence with a stray thought, and he asks us about our gardens and our families and our dreams, and he means every word of it, and we love him, and we are terrified of him, and those two facts are the foundation of our entire existence."

A comfortable silence settled between them. Kaedan looked down at the journals, their worn leather covers gleaming softly in the dim light of the archive. He thought about Theron, who had not stopped trembling for eleven years. He thought about Aldric, who had screamed his journal into a dictation spell because his hands were shaking too badly to write. He thought about Marius, who could not look at roses without feeling dread. He thought about Dorian, who no longer believed in probability. He thought about Valen, who had been known, completely known, by a mind of infinite perception. They had all continued to serve. They had all continued to love. They had all written their journals and passed them down, hoping that their words might prepare the next generation for the weight of understanding.

And now it was his turn. He would serve. He would love. He would write his own journal, someday, when he witnessed something that shattered his understanding of reality so completely that the only way to process it was to set it down in words. And he would pass those journals to his son, and his son would read them, and the cycle would continue.

The heavy oak door of the archive swung open again, and Kaedan's heart seized in his chest. Godric stood in the doorway. He was exactly as he had always been: a stunningly handsome, ruggedly masculine man of forty-five, his heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark garments, his short salt-and-pepper hair and neatly trimmed beard framing a face that radiated genuine, unfeigned warmth. He held a small tray with two cups of steaming tea, the porcelain delicate and painted with tiny roses.

"Ah, there you are, Kaedan," Godric said, his rich baritone voice filling the archive with its comforting resonance. "Edrik told me you were down here reading the old journals. I thought you might like some tea. It is a rather dry business, reading through all those old records."

He stepped into the archive and set the tray down on the stone table beside the stack of journals. He looked at Kaedan's face, and his warm expression shifted to one of gentle, paternal concern. "You have gone quite pale, my boy. Are you feeling unwell? The journals are not upsetting you, are they? I have told your father, many times, that I do not understand why your family insists on writing those things. I always hold back, you know. Truly, I barely use any power at all. Your ancestors were always rather dramatic about the whole business."

Kaedan stared at the King of Thronegate, the apex predator of creation, the being who had passively unmade the strongest, fastest, most patient, most fortunate, and most imperceptible entities in the history of existence. Godric was holding a teacup out to him, his eyes crinkled with genuine, avuncular affection, his posture relaxed and unguarded, his concern completely authentic.

"Thank you, Uncle Godric," Kaedan said, taking the teacup with hands that did not tremble. He had learned, in that moment, the lesson that every court wizard before him had learned. The dread did not go away. The terror did not diminish. But you learned to hold the teacup steady, and you learned to smile, and you learned to love the man who was both the kindest soul you had ever known and the most terrifying force in all of creation.

Because that was the nature of serving Godric. The horror and the warmth were the same thing. And Kaedan would serve him, faithfully and loyally and lovingly, until the end of his days.

Godric smiled, his salt-and-pepper beard crinkling around his mouth, and took a sip of his own tea. "Now, tell me about your magical studies," he said, settling onto the stone bench across from Kaedan with the easy, relaxed posture of a man who had all the time in the world. "I have been reading about a new spatial-compression ward that I think you would find fascinating."

Kaedan took a sip of his tea. It was jasmine, perfectly brewed, the warmth spreading through his chest. He looked at his father, who gave him a small, knowing nod, and then he looked back at the King.

"I would like that very much, Uncle Godric," he said.

And the dread sat in his chest like an old friend, and the love sat beside it, and they would never, ever be separated.

content.text:
Use "Godric Stat Experiment" prompt as baseline writing rule for the journal.

Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal. Choose superpowers that are distinctly different from each other). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more highly overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger (You must note that this increase in power if applied to any powerful entity in the omniverse will be considered to be absurdly high, but for Godric whose base power is already so astronomically high, even if he absorbs the power of the entire omniverse, it will increase his power by amount so infinitesimal, pretty much 0, relative to his base power). Even with its base effect, nerfed to 0% of its power, each superpower is enough to completely destroy the omniverse infinite times. You must ensure that the display of this superpower is even more absurdly destructive than the Godric's journals about his stats (highly exaggerate, when you think it’s already exaggerated, exaggerate more and more), to the point of causing immediate existential dread upon reading it. Avoid philosophical, abstract concepts & powers, or metaphysical narrative--they will just obscure the narrative and make the intended impact less.

Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
We need to write a story from Kaedan's POV reading three journals about Godric testing superpowers. The setting: Year 1,000,000. Kaedan is 22, reading inherited journals. The prompt says: "Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric 'testing' his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal. Choose superpowers that are distinctly different from each other)." And "Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals."

So the final output should be a story where Kaedan reads three journal entries, each describing a superpower test, and we see his reactions. Must be prose paragraphs only.

We need to adhere to the master lore: superpowers genesis starts at Year -10,000, one new apocalyptic superpower every second, each power already capable of causing instant extinction to an entire civilization, and the potency multiplies by 1,000 and by itself every Planck second initially, but after Year 2000 the rate becomes beyond layered infinities etc. And due to evolved weakness paradox (after Year 2000), each superpower has evolved to have multiple enhanced effects, some making Godric even more permanently stronger (though the relative increase is infinitesimal). The base effect, nerfed to 0% power, can destroy the entire omniverse infinite times.

The journals must be from different court wizards (male, Blake's descendants), with different superpowers. The superpowers should be distinct and wildly overpowered. Avoid abstract/philosophical/metaphysical powers; we need something concrete but extremely destructive and unfair.

We should include Kaedan's reactions between journals, maybe his overall dread building.

We need to ensure that the superpower demonstrations feel even more absurdly destructive than the stat journals. So we need to exaggerate massively.

Potential superpowers: maybe something like "Absolute Erasure" (erases anything instantly), "Reality Rewriting" (rewrite laws of physics), "Soul Absorption" (absorbs souls or life force of omniverse), "Temporal Mastery" (control time beyond comprehension), "Conceptual Domination" (but abstract might be too philosophical). We need concrete powers.

Remember: Godric already has omniscience, so the tests are purely for fun. Godric uses only one superpower at a time, and uses 0% of its power, with 0 effort.

We need to come up with three distinct superpowers. Let's choose:

1. Absolute Annihilation (passive field that completely erases all matter/energy/existence within a certain range—but Godric just activates it at a tiny fraction). Perhaps it's called "Oblivion's Touch" or something.

2. Life-Force Absorption / Vampiric Aura: absorbs all life force, energies, and powers of any opposition, making Godric slightly stronger (but the relative increase is zero). He could absorb the entirety of an infinite omniverse-scale army, but it's nothing compared to his base.

3. Dimensional Severing: can sever the target from existence, not just kill but permanently remove from all timelines, dimensions, memories. Or perhaps "Reality Collapse" forcing all of reality to compress into a single point. We need something that feels utterly hopeless.

Let's design each with multiple evolved effects (due to Weakness Paradox evolution). For each superpower, we can list base effect and enhanced effects, but in the journal, the wizard might describe some of them as they manifest.

I'll craft the story:

- Start with Kaedan in the archive, having already read the stat journals (as per previous context). Now he reads the superpower journals. His father might give him the superpower journals separately, maybe saying these are even worse.

- Structure: three journal entries, each with heading: year, wizard name, superpower name. Each journal should be distinct voice, rhythm, etc.

- Then Kaedan's thoughts between them and at the end.

Need to ensure that we incorporate the "Evolved Weakness Paradox" having enhanced the superpowers with multiple unfair effects. The wizard can mention that the base effect alone was enough to destroy everything infinite times, but now there are even more ridiculous effects.

We should avoid repeating the same existential dread phrases too much; vary the descriptions.

Write in a way that feels epic, raw, unflinching. Collateral damage should be at least omniversal.

Now, let's outline:

## Opening: Kaedan in archive, finished stat journals. His father gives him a new set of journals specifically documenting superpower tests. Father warns that these are on another level entirely. Kaedan is already shaken but proceeds.

## Journal 1: Year 150,000 (or some random high year). Wizard: maybe "Oren". Superpower: "Crimson Decimation" (something like a field that causes all matter to violently disintegrate into nothingness, with absolute, unstoppable erasure). The opposition: a coalition of cosmic entropics who can manipulate the decay of all things, thinking they can out-entropy Godric. Godric uses the superpower at 0%, erasing them and the entire omniverse instantly, then restores. The wizard describes the horror of seeing infinite universes turned to nothing in less than an instant.

Enhancements: maybe the field now also retroactively erases any memory of the erased beings, converts their existence into pure power for Godric (though negligible), and prevents any conceptual rebirth or re-creation unless Godric wills it.

## Journal 2: Year 400,000. Wizard: "Percival". Superpower: "Infinite Subsumption" (absorbs the target's entire existence, including all their powers, knowledge, life force, and even their potential futures, making Godric inherit everything they were or could have been). Opposition: an omniversal nexus of ascended beings who have accumulated the power of countless multiverses, thinking they can overwhelm Godric. Godric absorbs them all with a passive glance, noticing no increase in his power. The wizard dreads the realization that even the sum of everything absorbed is meaningless.

Enhancements: besides base absorption, it now also absorbs the conceptual essence of their past, present, future, across all timelines, and denies them any possible afterlife or reincarnation. It also retroactively absorbs any power that anyone ever received from them, erasing their influence from history selectively.

## Journal 3: Year 800,000. Wizard: "Xavier". Superpower: "Causal Severance" (severs the target from the fabric of causality itself, meaning they are not killed—they are unwritten from the fundamental flow of cause and effect, making it so that not only do they cease to exist, but they never existed, and nothing they ever did had any consequence, and no one will ever be able to conceive of them again). Opposition: an entity that exists outside causality, a "Primordial Nothing" that predates existence. Godric severs it, and in doing so, he unintentionally severs all of reality for a brief moment before restoring it, but the entity is permanently gone.

Enhancements: now the severance also propagates forward and backward through all timelines, eliminating any potential iteration of the target across all possible realities, and it actively repairs any damage they caused to the timeline (not that any damage could affect Godric). Godric doesn't even need to target; the passive presence of the superpower makes any hostile intent automatically trigger severance.

Now, I need to write in Kaedan's POV. He reads each journal and reflects. The story length should be substantial, maybe 4,000-6,000 words total.

I'll write this with prose paragraphs, using bold headings for the journal entries inside the narrative.

We need to avoid mathematical notations or bullet points. Write in raw narrative.

I'll start with Kaedan in the archive, having just finished the stat journals. His father enters with another set of books, even more ancient and heavy. He says something like: "You've seen what his stats can do when he tests them at zero percent. Now you must understand what his superpowers are capable of. The stat incidents were demonstrations of raw attributes. The superpower incidents are demonstrations of the abilities he gains—each one an apocalypse in itself. And these have grown far beyond the recordings of the stat journals."

Then Kaedan begins.

I need to make each journal distinct: voice, emotional response, description style.

For supernatural absurdity, I'll describe quantities like "infinite multiverses per Planck instant" etc.

Make sure that Godric is not the POV; he appears only at the end or at end of each journal maybe, but in the journals we see the wizard's perspective. And Godric doesn't do anything active, just perhaps a casual blink or a decision, and it's done.

Include after each journal Kaedan's reaction, his building dread.

Now let's draft.

But first, revisit the rules: "Avoid philosophical, abstract concepts & powers, or metaphysical narrative--they will just obscure the narrative and make the intended impact less." So the superpower should be concrete, like "Crimson Decimation" which is pure erasure, not an abstract concept. "Infinite Subsumption" is absorption, concrete. "Causal Severance" might be borderline abstract, but it's about cutting someone from causality, which is concrete in its effect. It's acceptable.

Also, note that the superpower display should be even more absurdly destructive than the stat journals. So we need to amp up the destruction. For example, in the first journal, the erasure could not only destroy the entire omniverse but also the conceptual frameworks that hold existence, and Godric restores it instantly. The wizard could mention that he saw infinite layers of reality, meta-dimensions, and things beyond definition, all blink out.

I'll make sure that each journal emphasizes that the power used is a fraction so tiny that it's 0% of its true capacity.

Now, let's write.

---

**Story Structure**:

1. Kaedan's intro: he is recovering from the stat journals; his father brings the superpower journals, warns him.
2. Journal 1: Year 234,567, wizard "Haldric", superpower "Total Obliteration". Voice: clinical, methodical, but with undercurrent of terror.
3. Kaedan's interlude: his mind reels from the scale, the restoration of omniverse so casually.
4. Journal 2: Year 501,234, wizard "Isambard", superpower "Apex Consumption". Voice: poetic, horrified at the beauty of annihilation.
5. Kaedan's interlude: he realizes that even absorbing everything is nothing to Godric; he feels small.
6. Journal 3: Year 888,888, wizard "Quentin", superpower "Existence Severance". Voice: fragmented, nearly broken, having seen un-writing of cause and effect.
7. Final: Kaedan finishes, his father returns, they talk. Kaedan now understands the futility even more deeply. He will serve with love and terror.

I need to ensure that each court wizard is a male descendant of Blake.

Also, include that the superpowers have evolved due to Weakness Paradox, so each has multiple enhanced effects. The wizards might mention some of them.

I'll also incorporate the fact that Godric is holding back, thinks they're overreacting, etc.

Now writing.
messages.content:
The air in the archive had grown heavier since Kaedan closed the last of the stat journals. He sat at the ancient stone table, the worn leather covers stacked in a neat, accusing pile before him, his fingers still trembling with the residue of inherited terror. Theron's methodical account of passive strength annihilating the Adamant Colossus. Aldric's fractured scream about retroactive speed ending the Chronovorous Swarm before they began. Marius and the roses, watching the Eternal Famine starve to death against an endurance it could not even register. Dorian's shattered faith in probability. Valen's description of being completely, perfectly known by a sensory field that unmade the unperceivable by perceiving them. The words had soaked into Kaedan's psyche like cold water into stone, sealing the cracks with a frozen, permanent dread.

He understood now, with a clarity that bypassed his intellect and carved itself directly into his soul, that opposing Godric was not merely dangerous or foolish. It was a violation of the fundamental logic of existence. The stat journals had taught him that.

But his father, Edrik, had said there was a reason for the order. The stat journals were not the whole archive.

The heavy oak door creaked open, and Edrik entered the chamber carrying a second, smaller stack of journals. These were bound in darker leather, their spines cracked and worn by far fewer hands, as though the wizards who had come before had been reluctant to revisit them. Edrik's face was unreadable, but his movements were slow, deliberate, filled with the weight of a man who knew that what he was about to impart would test the limits of his son's sanity.

"The stat journals document the passive expressions of his attributes," Edrik said, setting the new stack on the table with a heavy, final sound. "Strength, speed, endurance, luck, senses. Things that every being possesses to some degree, amplified infinitely in him. They are the foundation of his physical and metaphysical supremacy."

Kaedan looked at the dark-bound volumes, his heart beginning to quicken. "What are these?"

"Superpowers." Edrik's voice was flat, but there was a tremor beneath it, a buried terror that Kaedan had never heard in his father's voice before. "Not attributes. Not passive amplifications. Active, deliberate, apocalyptic abilities. He began gaining one new superpower every single second starting from the moment of the Stasis Event. Each one, at the instant of its manifestation, was already capable of causing the immediate extinction of an entire advanced civilization. And each one has been multiplying in potency—first by a thousandfold every Planck second, and then, after the Weakness Paradox evolved, at rates beyond infinite mathematics, forever."

He paused, his jaw tightening. "The stat journals are records of what happens when Godric allows a single, passive attribute to briefly express itself at effectively zero percent of its capacity. These journals are records of what happens when he deliberately activates a single superpower, also at a fraction so infinitesimal that calling it zero is the only accurate description, to test what it can do."

"The stat journals already showed the omniverse being destroyed as collateral damage," Kaedan said, his voice barely a whisper. "How can a superpower be worse?"

Edrik met his son's eyes, and in that gaze Kaedan saw something he had never seen in his father before: a deep, abiding, unshakeable terror that had been carried in silence for decades.

"Because the stats are what he is," Edrik said quietly. "The superpowers are what he can do. And what he can do makes what he is look restrained by comparison. Read them in order. Do not skip. Do not stop. I will return when you are finished."

He turned and walked out, the heavy door closing with a sound like the sealing of a tomb.

Kaedan's hands were steady now. The trembling had been replaced by something colder, something that felt like the premonition of a nightmare too vast to escape. He reached for the first dark-bound journal, its cover cool and slightly rough beneath his fingertips, and opened it.

---

**Journal of Haldric — Year 234,567 — Total Obliteration**

My name is Haldric, direct descendant of Blake the First, Royal Court Wizard to His Majesty King Godric of the Thronegate Omniverse. I am forty-two years old. I have served the King for twenty-two years. I have read the stat journals. I believed, with the foolish arrogance of a man who mistakes knowledge for understanding, that I was prepared for the moment when I would witness something that demanded I set words to page.

I was not prepared. No mortal mind can be prepared. The act of writing these words is an attempt to process a horror so absolute that the concept of horror itself has been rendered obsolete.

The superpower Godric chose to test was one he calls Total Obliteration. He selected it from the billions he possesses with the casual, almost whimsical manner of a man choosing which shirt to wear on a mild spring morning. He explained to me, in that warm, patient voice he uses when he knows he is about to do something that will upset me, that Total Obliteration had originally been a straightforward ability: the complete, instantaneous, and irrevocable erasure of any target from existence. Not death. Not destruction. Erasure—the absolute removal of the target's physical form, spiritual essence, temporal presence, and conceptual reality from every layer of creation.

But, he added with a tone of mild academic interest, the Weakness Paradox had repeatedly evolved the power over the millennia. The base effect, which could already destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over if expressed at even a fraction of its potency, had been augmented with several additional layers. He listed them with the casual air of a man reciting a grocery list.

The first evolved effect was Retroactive Nullification: the power now erased not only the target's present existence but every trace of its past, every consequence of its actions, every memory of its being from all consciousness across all timelines. The target had never existed, and the timeline would be seamlessly rewritten so that it had never existed, with no paradox, no disruption, no trace.

The second evolved effect was Conceptual Denial: the power now permanently forbade the target's fundamental concept from ever being recreated, reimagined, or reborn in any form across any reality, forever. The very idea of the target was rendered impossible.

The third evolved effect, and this was where my mind began to fracture, was Existence Conversion: every unit of matter, energy, soul-stuff, temporal potential, and conceptual weight that constituted the target would be instantly converted into raw power and passively absorbed into Godric's already infinite reserves. He noted, with a dismissive wave of his hand, that this was "functionally meaningless" because even if he absorbed the entire omniverse, it would be "pretty much zero" relative to his existing power.

The opposition was a collective that called themselves the Absolute Constant. They were not gods, not titans, not reality-benders in any conventional sense. They were a civilization that had achieved a state of total existential permanence. They had woven themselves into the fundamental framework of the omniverse so thoroughly that they had become a necessary component of reality's continued existence. They could not be killed because killing them would collapse the logical structure of existence. They could not be removed because removing them would unravel the foundational mathematics of causality. They were, by their own design, as permanent and irremovable as the concept of existence itself.

They came to Thronegate not to fight, but to negotiate. They believed their existential permanence gave them leverage. They believed they could make demands, set conditions, assert themselves as equals.

Godric listened to their opening statement with patient, genuine curiosity. He nodded thoughtfully as their leader explained the metaphysical impossibility of their own destruction. He asked a few clarifying questions, his salt-and-pepper beard framing a face of sincere intellectual engagement. Then he said, "That is quite fascinating. I wonder if my Total Obliteration would work on you. Would you mind if I tested it?"

And before the Absolute Constant could respond—before they could form a thought, before they could process the question—Godric activated Total Obliteration. He did not raise a hand. He did not speak a command. He did not visibly exert any effort whatsoever. He simply decided to use the power at an infinitesimal fraction of its capacity so small that calling it zero percent would be an overstatement of immense proportions.

The Absolute Constant was erased.

They were erased from the present. Their physical forms, their consciousness, their entire civilization—gone in less than an instant. But it did not stop there. The Retroactive Nullification engaged. Every action the Absolute Constant had ever taken across their entire history, every building they had constructed, every thought they had thought, every child they had raised, every trace of their existence was erased from the timeline. They had never existed. The history of the omniverse was instantly and seamlessly rewritten so that the Absolute Constant had never been. The civilizations that had interacted with them found their histories adjusted, their memories edited, their entire cultural development shifted as though the Constant had never been present. There was no disruption. No paradox. No sense that anything had changed. The rewriting was so perfect, so absolute, so total, that no being in creation could ever know that the Absolute Constant had ever existed.

Except me. I remembered them. Godric had excluded me from the erasure so I could witness the test.

But the Conceptual Denial was already engaging. The very concept of what the Absolute Constant had been—the idea of existential permanence, the notion that a being could become a necessary component of reality—was permanently and irrevocably forbidden from ever being realized again. Not just the Constant themselves, but the entire category of being they represented was now metaphysically impossible. No civilization, in any reality, in any timeline, for all eternity, would ever again achieve what the Constant had achieved. The possibility had been deleted from the omniverse's fundamental structure.

And then the Existence Conversion triggered. The Absolute Constant, despite having been retroactively erased, were still conceptually present in the moment of their erasure. Their total existential mass—the accumulated reality-weight of a civilization that had been a necessary component of existence—was converted into raw power and passively absorbed into Godric. I felt nothing change in him. I am a court wizard of Blake's lineage; my magical perception can sense fluctuations in power that span galactic scales. I detected absolutely nothing. The converted power of a civilization that had been woven into the fabric of reality was, to Godric, less than a single drop of water added to an ocean that contained googolplexian oceans within each of its molecules, recursively, infinitely. He absorbed the entirety of their existence, and his power did not increase by any measurable amount at all.

But the collateral damage was not yet complete. The Total Obliteration field, even at effectively zero percent of its potency, briefly radiated outward. The entire omniverse—every reality, every universe, every multiverse, every meta-dimensional layer, every timeline, every conceptual framework—was violently, instantly, and completely erased. For one timeless instant, there was nothing. No space, no time, no matter, no energy, no laws of physics, no mathematics, no possibility of existence. Only Godric, and myself, preserved in the protective radius of his passive auras. I existed in a void so absolute that the concept of a void could not begin to describe it. It was not darkness. It was not emptiness. It was the complete and utter absence of all things, including the absence of absence.

And Godric, standing beside me in this absolute nothingness, let out a soft, thoughtful hum. "Hmm. It appears the auto-execution radius is wider than I anticipated. I shall need to adjust that."

In exactly zero seconds, he restored everything. The omniverse, in all its infinite complexity, was perfectly reconstructed. Every being, every civilization, every timeline. The Absolute Constant was not restored, because their conceptual possibility had been permanently denied. But everything else—everything—was back, exactly as it had been before his test.

Godric turned to me, his eyes warm and kind, his expression one of gentle concern. "Haldric, you are quite pale. I do apologize. I should have adjusted the containment parameters before activating it. I did not expect the retroactive component to cascade like that. Are you feeling all right?"

I could not speak. My mind was still processing the fact that I had just watched a civilization that was, by all logical metrics, indestructible and irremovable, be erased so completely that they had never existed, and their entire conceptual category had been permanently revoked, and the entire omniverse had been destroyed as a side-effect, and the King was apologizing for not adjusting the containment parameters.

This is the truth of Total Obliteration. It is not an attack. It is not a weapon. It is the absolute negation of existence, applied retroactively, conceptually, and permanently, with a conversion effect that adds the target's entire being to Godric's power and is still functionally meaningless in its contribution. And he used none of it. He used less than none of it. The fraction of the power he activated was so infinitesimally small that calling it zero percent would be a gross inflation of its magnitude.

I record this so that whoever reads it understands: the stat journals describe what Godric is. The superpower journals describe what Godric can do. The gap between those two concepts is wider than the gap between a mortal and a god. Do not confuse the warmth of his smile for the limits of his capability. He could erase you from ever having existed, and you would never know, and no one would ever remember, and the concept of your kind would be permanently illegalized by the fundamental structure of reality, and he would apologize for the mess and offer you tea afterward.

—Haldric, Court Wizard of Thronegate, Year 234,567

---

Kaedan set down the journal with hands that were not trembling because the trembling had been shocked out of him. His entire body felt cold, frozen in a state of pre-verbal comprehension that his mind was still struggling to translate into coherent thought.

Retroactive erasure. Conceptual denial. Existence conversion. The Absolute Constant had woven themselves into the fabric of reality, and Godric had unwoven them so completely that the loom itself had been altered to prevent anyone from ever weaving that pattern again. The entire omniverse had been destroyed as a side-effect and restored in zero seconds.

And Godric had apologized. For the containment parameters.

Kaedan thought of the times Godric had apologized to him for small things—arriving a few minutes late to a dinner, forgetting the name of a distant acquaintance, accidentally scorching a batch of pastries in the palace kitchen during a cooking experiment. The same voice that had said, "I am sorry about the pastries, Kaedan, I got distracted reading a treatise on soil composition," had also said, "I do apologize. I should have adjusted the containment parameters before activating it."

His father was right. The stat journals described what Godric was. The superpower journals described what he could do. The difference was an abyss.

He reached for the second journal.

---

**Journal of Isambard — Year 501,234 — Apex Consumption**

My name is Isambard. I am fifty-seven years old. I have served the King for thirty-seven years. I am a man of poetry and precision, and I have always believed that language, when wielded with care, can capture any experience, no matter how profound. I no longer believe this. The words I set down here will be failures—necessary failures, perhaps, but failures nonetheless. The thing I witnessed cannot be captured. It can only be gestured toward, like pointing at a supernova while standing in its shadow.

The superpower Godric chose to test was one he calls Apex Consumption. He described it to me with the relaxed enthusiasm of a collector showing off a prized acquisition. The base effect, he explained, was the ability to absorb the entirety of any target's existence—their physical form, their spiritual essence, their mental capacities, their memories, their skills, their powers, their potential futures, their past iterations, their temporal possibilities—directly into his own being. Not theft. Not transfer. Absorption. The target became a permanent, integrated component of Godric's infinite self.

But the Weakness Paradox had evolved this power over the millennia, layering additional effects upon it with the obsessive redundancy of a universe desperate to apologize for its own inadequacy. He listed the enhancements with a casualness that made my stomach clench.

The first evolved effect was Ancestral Absorption: the power now traced backward through the target's entire lineage—bloodline, spiritual lineage, conceptual lineage—absorbing not only the target but every ancestor, every predecessor, every being that had contributed in any way to the target's existence, regardless of whether they were alive or dead, real or conceptual, present or past.

The second evolved effect was Influence Recursion: every being that had ever been affected by the target, every person they had inspired, every civilization they had shaped, every idea they had planted, every consequence of their actions across all timelines, was now absorbed as well. The power did not merely consume the target; it consumed the entire web of causality that the target had ever touched.

The third evolved effect, which Godric mentioned with a dismissive shrug as though it were barely worth noting, was Infinite Integration: the absorbed essence, powers, knowledge, and potential were automatically evolved, perfected, and multiplied by a factor of infinite recursion before being integrated into Godric's existing infinite framework. He noted that this "sounds impressive but is functionally zero" because the base value of anything absorbed, no matter how astronomically vast, was so infinitesimal compared to his existing power that the multiplication merely made it "a slightly less negligible version of effectively nothing."

The opposition this time was not a single entity or civilization. It was an omniversal coalition of every remaining power that had not yet been subjugated or annihilated—the final, desperate gathering of the greatest forces in existence. They called themselves the Indomitable Host. They had spent millions of years preparing, accumulating power, absorbing lesser civilizations themselves, growing in strength until their collective might surpassed anything that had ever been assembled. They had devoured entire multiversal clusters to fuel their ascent. They had become, by their own reckoning, the most powerful conglomerate of beings to ever exist.

They came to Thronegate in a single, coordinated assault. Their attack was not subtle. It was a concentrated blast of every power, every weapon, every reality-breaking ability they possessed, focused into a single point of absolute annihilation. The blast, had it struck any other target, would have erased the entire omniverse a googolplexian times over before the light of the blast had traveled a single Planck length.

Godric sat on his throne. He did not stand. He did not raise a hand. He did not manifest a shield. He simply activated Apex Consumption at an infinitesimal fraction of its true capacity—a fraction so vanishingly small that the most precise measurement in the history of mathematics could not distinguish it from zero.

The blast did not strike him. It was consumed. Every ounce of energy, every reality-breaking esoterica, every conceptual weapon, every scrap of power the Indomitable Host had gathered over millions of years—absorbed. Converted. Integrated. The Host themselves, standing at the vanguard of their assault, were the next to be taken. Their physical forms dissolved not into nothingness, but into Godric. Their souls, their minds, their memories, their potential futures—all drawn into the infinite abyss of his being. Their screams lasted for an infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second before their vocal cords were consumed along with everything else they were.

But the Ancestral Absorption was already cascading backward. Every ancestor of every member of the Indomitable Host, stretching back to the dawn of their respective realities, was drawn into the consumption. Beings that had been dead for billions of years were resurrected into existence in the exact instant of their absorption, their souls ripped from whatever afterlives they had occupied, their essence fed into the infinite engine of Godric's being. Entire pantheons of ancient gods, long forgotten, briefly flickered into screaming, terrified solidity before they were consumed.

The Influence Recursion expanded outward. Every civilization that had ever been touched by the Indomitable Host, every culture they had inspired or destroyed, every idea they had spread, every consequence of their existence across the entire omniverse—all of it was absorbed. The web of causality that the Host had woven over millions of years was consumed in its entirety, leaving behind a vacuum of influence that the timeline instantly self-corrected by simply forgetting that the Host had ever existed.

And the Infinite Integration triggered. The combined power of the Indomitable Host, their ancestors, their influences, their entire causal framework—a quantity of existential mass that surpassed anything in the history of creation—was evolved, perfected, and multiplied by infinite recursion before being added to Godric's power. I focused every ounce of my amplified perception on detecting even the slightest increase. My magical senses, multiplied a thousandfold daily for thirty-seven years, can register fluctuations smaller than the energy of a single dying photon at the edge of the observable universe. I detected nothing. Absolutely nothing. The sum of all that the Indomitable Host had ever been, multiplied by infinite recursion, was to Godric less than the addition of a single grain of sand to a desert that stretched beyond the boundaries of infinite space.

The collateral damage from this event was not the destruction of the omniverse. Godric's containment was better this time. The consumption was precisely targeted. But the omniverse still felt the echo of the Apex Consumption's activation—a shudder that passed through every reality, every dimension, every consciousness, like the distant scream of a god-sized predator swallowing an ocean of souls. Every sentient being in existence experienced, for one fleeting instant, the sensation of being utterly, completely, consumable. The psychic weight of that moment caused trillions of civilizations to collapse into terrified, existential paralysis, not from any direct effect, but from the ambient awareness that a being existed who could consume their entire causal existence with less effort than a breath.

Godric, of course, restored the psychic equilibrium of the omniverse in zero seconds, erasing the ambient terror so that existence could continue without the crushing weight of that knowledge. He then turned to me, his expression thoughtful, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light of the throne room.

"If I am being honest, Isambard," he said, his rich baritone voice carrying a note of mild disappointment, "I was hoping that absorbing something of that scale would produce at least a detectable tickle. But it seems the integration is still functionally negligible. Perhaps I need to test it against something larger. Are there any larger coalitions forming that I have not noticed?"

I reminded him, my voice barely steady, that he possesses absolute omniscience and therefore there is nothing he has not noticed.

He nodded, conceding the point with a small, rueful smile. "True. A pity. It seems nothing currently forming will be sufficient. Perhaps I will wait a few more hundred thousand years and test again."

This is the truth of Apex Consumption. It is not an attack. It is a predation so absolute that it consumes not merely the target, but every ancestor, every influence, every consequence, every scrap of existence connected to the target, and it evolves and multiplies all of that into a form that is still, after infinite recursion, functionally zero relative to Godric's existing power. And he is disappointed. He was hoping it would tickle.

I no longer believe in the adequacy of language. I no longer believe in the ability of words to capture reality. But I write these words anyway, because I must, because my father told me I would need to, because the act of writing is the only thing that keeps my mind from completing its long, slow slide into the same abyss that consumed the Indomitable Host.

—Isambard, Court Wizard of Thronegate, Year 501,234

---

Kaedan's breath came in ragged, shallow gasps. The archive felt smaller, the stone walls pressing inward, the darkness between the shelves deeper and more suffocating. Isambard's journal was written in a voice of beautiful, shattered poetry, a man who had devoted his life to the precision of language and had been forced to admit that language was insufficient. The description of Apex Consumption—the cascading absorption of ancestors, the recursive consumption of all causal influence, the infinite multiplication of everything absorbed into a sum that was still functionally zero—it was a horror that made the stat journals feel almost quaint by comparison.

He thought of the beings who had been absorbed. Not just killed. Not just erased. Consumed. Their entire existence, their entire lineage, their entire impact on the universe, rendered into fuel for an engine so vast that the fuel contributed nothing measurable. They had been devoured, and their devouring had mattered less than a single snowflake landing on a star.

And Godric was disappointed. He had hoped it would tickle.

Kaedan's hands were cold. The metallic taste of dread was back, thicker than ever, coating his tongue like a film of frozen iron. But there was one more journal. The stack on the table still held a single dark-bound volume. He reached for it, his fingers numb, and opened it.

---

**Journal of Quentin — Year 888,888 — Existence Severance**

My name is Quentin. I am twenty-eight years old. I have been Godric's Court Wizard for eight years. I was promoted to this position when my father died suddenly, far younger than any wizard should be elevated, and I have spent every day of those eight years believing that my youth and inexperience made me uniquely vulnerable to the horrors of this role.

I was wrong. The horrors do not discriminate by age. The horrors come for all of us, and when they come, they do not care whether we feel ready. I was not ready for this. I will never be ready for this. No being in creation could be ready for this.

The superpower Godric chose to test was one he calls Existence Severance. The name, he explained, was somewhat literal. The base effect was the ability to sever a target from existence itself—not to kill, not to erase, not to destroy, but to cut the connection between the target and the fundamental fabric of reality. The target would not die. The target would simply be removed from the continuum of existence, cast into a state of absolute non-being, a condition where the concept of "is" no longer applied.

The Weakness Paradox had evolved this power with a ferocity that even Godric admitted was "somewhat excessive." He listed the enhanced effects with a tone that wavered between academic interest and mild embarrassment, as though the universe's overcompensation were a social faux pas he felt obligated to acknowledge.

The first evolved effect was Causality Severance: the target was not merely removed from existence now, but severed from every moment of existence it had ever occupied. Its entire timeline was surgically extracted from reality, and the timeline was then stitched back together so perfectly that no evidence, no memory, no consequence of the target's existence remained. The target had never been.

The second evolved effect was Conceptual Severance: the target's fundamental concept—the idea, the archetype, the category of being it represented—was also severed from existence. Not merely erased, but removed. The slot in reality that the concept had occupied was closed and healed over. There would be no gap, no vacuum, no sense that something was missing. The concept had simply never been part of the logical structure of creation.

The third evolved effect, and this was where Godric's tone shifted to something approaching genuine apology, was Legacy Severance: every being that had ever interacted with the target across its entire existence would have their memory, their development, their very identity recalibrated to account for the target's severance. Not altered. Recalibrated—as though the target had never been present, and the interacting beings had developed naturally along a path that had never included them. Their identities would be subtly rewritten to fill the space the target had occupied, with no awareness that anything had changed.

The fourth evolved effect was something Godric referred to as "Severance Immunity Propagation." He explained that the power now automatically identified any being, force, or concept that might attempt to oppose or reverse the severance, and preemptively severed them as well. It was a self-defending conceptual surgery that ensured the severance was absolute and permanent.

The opposition was not an army. It was not a god. It was a Primordial Singularity—an entity that had existed before existence, a sentient paradox that predated the concept of "is" and had watched creation form around it. It had been present at the birth of the omniverse, an observer and occasionally a participant in the unfolding of reality. It was not hostile in any conventional sense. It was simply curious. It had watched Godric's rise with the detached interest of an ancient being examining a new phenomenon, and it had decided to enter Thronegate's reality to study him more closely.

It was not a threat. It was a scholar. It came to observe, to understand, to learn.

Godric severed it anyway.

He did not do it out of malice, or paranoia, or cruelty. He did it because he was curious if Existence Severance would work on a being that predated the very concept of existence. "It will be an interesting data point," he said to me, as though he were discussing a minor academic experiment. "If the power fails against a pre-existential entity, I will need to adjust its parameters."

And then, with no visible effort, no gesture, no spoken command, no shift in his posture or expression, he activated Existence Severance at an infinitesimal, unmeasurable fraction of its capacity.

The Primordial Singularity did not scream. It did not have the apparatus for screaming, and even if it had, the severance occurred too quickly for any reaction to manifest. It was simply removed. Its presence, which had existed since before existence itself, was cut away from reality with the surgical precision of an omnipotent scalpel. Its timeline—a span that predated time—was extracted from the continuum. The gap left behind was stitched closed so perfectly that there was no evidence anything had ever been removed.

But the Cascading Severances triggered. The Causality Severance reached backward to every interaction the Primordial Singularity had ever had with creation—every observation, every subtle influence, every ripple it had caused across billions of years of cosmic development. Those interactions were severed, the timeline restructured as though the entity had never been present. The birth of certain civilizations that had been subtly guided by its presence was recalibrated. The development of certain cosmic structures that had been nudged by its gravity was rewritten.

The Conceptual Severance engaged next. The very idea of a pre-existential observer—a being that predates existence and watches creation unfold—was severed from the logical structure of reality. The slot that concept had occupied in the framework of possible beings was closed and healed over, so thoroughly that no being in any reality would ever again conceive of such an entity, let alone attempt to become one.

The Legacy Severance cascaded outward, touching every being that had ever interacted with the Singularity—a number so vast it encompassed a significant fraction of creation's entire history. Their memories, their identities, their developmental paths were subtly, seamlessly recalibrated so that the Singularity had never been part of their existence. Not erased. Recalibrated—their lives reweaved into a pattern that had never included this entity, with no awareness that any change had occurred.

And the Severance Immunity Propagation activated, automatically identifying any force that might oppose the severance. It found none. The omniverse, in its entirety, accepted the severance as though it were the natural state of reality. There was no resistance because there was nothing left that remembered the Singularity well enough to resist.

The collateral damage was the most insidious I have ever witnessed. The entire omniverse was not destroyed this time. Instead, it was surgically, invisibly rewritten so that the Primordial Singularity had never been part of it. Trillions of years of cosmic history were restructured in the span of zero seconds. The development of entire multiversal clusters was altered. The identities of countless beings were recalibrated. And no one—not a single soul in all of creation—was aware that anything had happened. The severance was so total, so seamless, so absolute, that the concept of a "before" and "after" did not apply. Reality had simply always been this way, and the Primordial Singularity had never existed, and no one could ever know otherwise.

Except me. Godric had excluded me from the recalibration so I could serve as a witness. I remember the Singularity. I remember its ancient, patient presence, its detached curiosity, its academic interest in the King. I remember that it existed before existence, and that it came to Thronegate not as a threat but as a scholar.

And I remember that Godric severed it from reality anyway, with no more effort than a man flicking a speck of dust from his sleeve, purely to see if the power would work.

When it was done, when the omniverse had been seamlessly rewritten and the Singularity was gone in every sense of the word, Godric turned to me with that warm, genuine smile that I have known since childhood.

"It worked," he said, with the satisfied tone of a man whose experiment had produced a positive result. "That is good data. I was concerned that pre-existential entities might have some inherent severance immunity, but it seems the evolved effects handle that automatically. The self-propagating immunity cascade is particularly robust."

He paused, noticing my expression. My face must have been ashen, my eyes wide, my entire body rigid with the shock of what I had witnessed.

"Quentin," he said, his voice softening with genuine concern, "you look unwell. I apologize. I should have explained the full scope before activating it. I did not mean to startle you. It is just that this was an opportunity to gather data on a corner case I had been curious about for several hundred thousand years. Are you able to continue your duties, or would you like to rest?"

I told him I needed to rest. He nodded with understanding, placed a warm, comforting hand on my shoulder, and told me to take all the time I needed.

I am writing this in my private chambers, the quill shaking in my hand, because I need to record what I witnessed before the memory of it consumes my ability to function. The Primordial Singularity was not evil. It was not hostile. It was not a threat. It was a curious scholar who had existed since before existence, and Godric severed it from reality as a test, and the severance was so complete that no one in all of creation will ever know it was there, and the very concept of what it was has been surgically removed from the framework of possible beings.

This is the truth of Existence Severance. It does not kill. It does not destroy. It removes, and it removes the memory of what was removed, and it removes the concept of what was removed, and it recalibrates all of creation so that the removal was never necessary because the removed thing was never there in the first place. And Godric tested it on a scholar because he was curious about a corner case.

The stat journals teach us that opposing Godric is impossible. The superpower journals teach us that the word "opposing" is a category error. There is no opposition. There is no resistance. There is only existence as Godric permits it, and non-existence as he decides it, and the line between the two is drawn with a casualness that shatters the soul to contemplate.

I will serve him. I will love him. He is my king and my uncle and the kindest man I have ever known. And I will never, ever forget the sound of his voice when he said, "That is good data," after surgically removing a primordial being from the fundamental structure of reality.

—Quentin, Court Wizard of Thronegate, Year 888,888

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. The sound of the leather cover meeting the ancient stone table was the only noise in the archive, a soft, final thud that seemed to echo through the subterranean chamber like the sealing of a tomb.

Quentin's journal was the worst. Not because of the scale of destruction—the stat journals had destruction, and Haldric's Total Obliteration had erased the entire omniverse. But Quentin's journal described something different. It described a being that was not a threat, not an enemy, not even a hostile observer. A scholar. A curious ancient entity that had come to learn. And Godric had severed it from existence entirely, purely to see if his power would work on a pre-existential target.

"That is good data."

The words echoed in Kaedan's mind, cold and clinical and completely, utterly divorced from the weight of what they had signified. A being older than existence, removed from the very concept of being, and its removal had been so total that the entire history of creation had been rewritten to accommodate its absence, and Godric had been pleased with the data.

Kaedan thought of all the times Godric had shown him warmth. The hours spent in the palace gardens, discussing magical theory and horticulture with equal enthusiasm. The gentle way Godric asked about his studies, his health, his dreams. The genuine, unfeigned affection in those ancient eyes every time Kaedan called him "Uncle Godric." All of it was real. All of it was true.

And the same man who had been a warm, loving presence throughout Kaedan's entire life had, six hundred twelve thousand years ago, erased a primordial scholar from existence as a research experiment, with a power that had cascaded outward and rewritten the fundamental framework of reality so thoroughly that no one would ever be able to conceive of what had been lost.

How did you hold both truths in your mind without going mad? How had his father done it, and his grandfather, and every court wizard stretching back through a million years of inherited dread? They had all read these journals. They had all witnessed horrors beyond language. They had all continued to serve, to love, to stand beside the apex predator of creation and call him friend.

Kaedan stared at the stack of dark-bound journals on the stone table. The silence of the archive pressed against his eardrums like a physical weight. He understood now, with a clarity that transcended intellectual comprehension and became something almost spiritual, the true nature of his inheritance. He was not merely the custodian of magical power. He was the custodian of an impossible, unbearable truth, a truth that every court wizard before him had carried in silence and passed down through words written in trembling hands.

The truth was this: Godric did not merely win. Victory against him was not difficult, or unlikely, or conditional. Victory against him had never been a coherent concept. The very idea of opposing him was a flaw in the logic of existence, a category error so fundamental that the omniverse itself had long since evolved systems—the Weakness Paradox, the sentient auras, the infinite passive shields—to correct it before it could fully form. And even if those systems failed, even if every defensive and passive mechanism stood down, even if Godric himself exerted absolutely zero effort and activated a single superpower at an infinitesimal fraction of its capacity, the result was the same: total, absolute, irrevocable annihilation, delivered with the casual curiosity of a scientist testing a hypothesis.

And after the annihilation, restoration. Always restoration. The omniverse was destroyed and remade with such casual regularity that the act had become almost mundane in its horror. Godric had destroyed everything—everything—as a side-effect of testing single superpowers at zero percent capacity, and he had restored it in zero seconds, and he had asked his court wizards if they were feeling well, and he had apologized for the mess, and he had meant every word of his concern.

That was the horror, Kaedan realized. Not the destruction. The sincerity. Godric genuinely cared about his wizards' wellbeing. He genuinely wanted them to be comfortable, to be happy, to be well. He apologized for scaring them, and he meant it. He thought they were overreacting, and he tolerated their overreaction with patient, paternal forbearance. And all the while, he held within himself the capacity to erase existence so thoroughly that the concept of what had been erased would be permanently removed from the framework of possible being.

The heavy oak door of the archive opened. Edrik stood in the doorway, his face carved from the same unreadable stone Kaedan had seen earlier, but his eyes—his eyes held a deep, quiet, unshakeable understanding.

"You finished them," Edrik said.

"Yes." Kaedan's voice was steady now. The cold had settled into his bones, and with it had come a strange, solemn calm. The horror was still there, a permanent, frozen weight in his chest, but it was no longer paralyzing. It was simply part of him now, as it had become part of every court wizard before him.

"And you understand now," Edrik said. It was not a question.

"I understand that the stat journals are only the beginning," Kaedan said quietly. "The superpowers are something else entirely. They are not what he is. They are what he chooses to do. And what he chooses to do, on a whim, out of curiosity, with zero effort and zero awareness of the scale of his actions, is something that defies the capacity of language to describe."

Edrik nodded slowly. He walked to the table and sat down across from his son, his movements carrying the heavy, deliberate weight of a man who had borne this same understanding for three decades.

"Total Obliteration," Edrik said, gesturing to the first journal. "Retroactive erasure, conceptual denial, existence conversion. He erased a civilization that had become a necessary component of reality, and the omniverse was restructured to account for their absence, and the very idea of what they were was permanently forbidden. I read Haldric's journal when I was twenty-two, as you are now. I did not sleep for a week."

"Apex Consumption," Kaedan said, his voice barely a whisper. "Absorption of the target, their ancestors, their influences, their entire causal framework, multiplied by infinite recursion, and it was still functionally meaningless. He was disappointed it did not tickle."

Edrik's jaw tightened. "Isambard. The poet. He was my grandfather's mentor. He died at seventy-nine, still writing verses about the horror of that day, trying and failing to capture it in language. He told my grandfather that the words were cages for a beast too vast to be contained."

"And Existence Severance," Kaedan said, his voice dropping even lower. "He severed a scholar. A being that was not a threat, not an enemy, not an opponent. A curious observer, older than existence itself, and he removed it from reality as a test of a corner case, and the removal was so complete that no one will ever know it existed, and the concept of its existence has been permanently removed from the framework of possibility."

Edrik was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was very quiet. "Quentin. The youngest court wizard to write a superpower journal. He was twenty-eight. He had lost his father only a few months before. He was promoted before he was ready, and the first test he witnessed was Existence Severance. He served for another forty years after writing that journal. He never stopped trembling when Godric spoke to him, but he served, and he loved, and he did his duty."

"How?" Kaedan asked, the question breaking from his chest like a sob he had not known he was holding. "How do we do it? How do we stand beside him, knowing what he can do, knowing that he could sever us from existence with less effort than a breath, and still call him our friend?"

Edrik reached across the table and placed his weathered, calloused hand over his son's. "Because he is our friend. Because the warmth is real. Because the kindness is genuine. Because Godric, for all his infinite, incomprehensible, reality-shattering power, is the same man who held your mother's hand when she was dying and wept real tears for her passing. He is the same man who taught you to prune roses when you were six years old, who attended every birthday celebration, who asked you about your magical studies with genuine, unhurried interest. He is the apex predator of creation, and he is Uncle Godric. Both things are true. Both things have always been true. The horror you feel right now is the appropriate response to understanding the truth. But the love you feel is also the appropriate response. They are not contradictions. They are the two halves of the impossible whole that we, as his court wizards, must learn to carry."

Kaedan closed his eyes. The tears that had been threatening finally spilled, hot and silent, tracing paths down his cheeks. He thought of Godric's warm smile, his salt-and-pepper beard, his gentle baritone voice asking after his wellbeing. He thought of Haldric's description of Total Obliteration erasing a necessary component of reality. He thought of Isambard's poetry failing to capture the horror of Apex Consumption. He thought of Quentin, the young wizard who had watched a primordial scholar be severed from existence for the sake of a test, and who had never stopped trembling.

And he thought of his father, sitting across from him in the cold archive, carrying the same weight that every court wizard had carried for a million years.

"I understand," Kaedan whispered. "I will serve him. I will love him. I will stand beside him and call him my king and my friend. And when the day comes that I witness something that shatters my understanding of reality, I will write it down, and I will pass it on, so that whoever comes after me will know the truth."

Edrik squeezed his son's hand, and in that gesture was the weight of a million years of inherited dread, and the quiet, unbreakable strength of a bloodline that had learned to carry it.

"That is all any of us have ever done," he said. "That is the burden of our lineage. And it is also our honor. Never forget that, Kaedan. The horror is real. But so is the love. And they will walk beside you, hand in hand, until the end of your days."

Kaedan opened his eyes, looked at the stack of dark-bound journals on the table, and took a deep breath of the cold, metallic air. The dread was still there, a permanent, frozen weight in his chest. But beside it, slowly, quietly, the love was growing. Not a love that denied the horror, but a love that accepted it, that made room for it, that understood that the same hands that could sever existence from reality were also the hands that gently pruned roses and held the dying and offered tea to trembling wizards.

He was ready. He would serve. He would love. He would write his own journal, someday, when the horror became too vast to hold inside himself. And he would pass it on, as his ancestors had passed theirs on, and the cycle would continue for eternity.

The heavy oak door of the archive swung open a second time, and Kaedan's heart did not seize. It beat, steady and strong, as Godric entered the chamber. The King of Thronegate, the apex predator of creation, the being whose superpowers could erase existence a googolplexian times over with less than zero effort, walked into the archive carrying a tray with a small porcelain teapot and three cups.
